pattern: Because the same codec instance handles both ends of a single request, an in-memory table keyed by a freshly minted short code is all the state needed to invert the mapping — nothing about the URL's contents has to be understood at all.
hints: The interesting part is not compressing the URL — it is inventing a short key you can reliably map back to the exact original string later.
Keep a dictionary from generated short code to the original URL inside the codec instance; encode and decode are always called on that same instance for a given request.

A monotonically increasing counter (or a random token) turned into a short string is enough for the key — store the full URL as the value and look it up verbatim on decode, with no parsing of the URL itself.

edge_inputs.description: very long URL near the length constraint
trailing slash and mixed case must be preserved exactly, not normalized
percent-encoded (unicode-safe ASCII) characters embedded in the path and query
URL already carrying a fragment and something that looks like a short code
distinct URL sharing a domain with an earlier case, to confirm each case is judged independently

stress.description: random-length URL-shaped strings drawn from a URL-safe alphabet
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(5, max(6, min(size, 400)))
    chars = 'abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyzABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ0123456789-_./~%?=&'
    path = ''.join(rng.choice(chars) for _ in range(n))
    return ['https://example.com/' + path]
